original_abstract: This article provides a critical evaluation of the diverse conceptualizations of illiberalism, proposing a refined definition that positions the concept as the direct negation of three foundational liberal democratic principles: limited power, state neutrality, and an open society. By shifting the focus from specific institutional failures to a broader ideational syndrome, the study clarifies the complex relationship between illiberalism, populism, and authoritarianism. The second part of the article examines the implications of this framework for empirical research and identifies nine distinct "roads to illiberalism"—authoritarian, traditionalist, religious, libertarian, nativist-nationalist, populist, paternalist, materialist-technocratic, and left-wing—demonstrating how varied ideological motives can converge in their opposition to the liberal democratic vision.
humanized_abstract: This article explores the various meanings that have been assigned to illiberalism in current debate. By defining illiberalism more closely and precisely in relation to the principles of liberal democratic governance as a concept that negates the three classical restrictions of politics—coup d’état or political disenfranchisement, censorship or discrimina-tion, and social closure—it clarifies the link between illiberalism, populism, and authori-tarianism. The second part focuses on the empirical research implications of this analytical definition, revealing that in different countries there are vastly different—and in many respects mutually exclusive—“roads to illiberalism”, depending on the variant of illiberalism that is implemented. Such varied ideas become compatible when they converge in relation to their fundamentally antagonistic relationship with liberal democracy. Thus, the author identifies nine variants of illiberalism as “roads to illiberalism”—authoritarian, traditionalist, religious, libertarian, nativist-nationalist, populist, paternalist, materialist-technocratic and left-wing.
